Core Narrative & Theme: Trees, especially evergreens and the mythical Gaokerena, are symbols of immortality and the divine (Ameretát). The pomegranate and cypress are especially sacred. The legendary Cypress of Kashmar was planted by Zoroaster. The Gaokerena is a cosmic tree that bestows immortality and is protected by divine beings12345.
Notes / Comparative Motifs: Cypress: axis mundi, immortality; Gaokerena: master of all plants, revives the dead; pomegranate: sustenance, fertility.

Name of the Tree (Language): Tree of Life (Greek: ξύλον της ζωής, Chinese: 龍樹)
Core Narrative & Theme: The Tree of Life grows in the Kingdom of Light, with three trunks symbolizing the cosmic directions. It is venerated as a source of salvation and the liberation of Light from matter. Manichaean iconography often depicts the tree as the axis mundi and as a scene of worship611.
Key Figures / Deities: Father of Majesty, Mother of Life, Mani
Primary Source(s) & Citations: Bezeklik Caves murals; Manichaean psalms; Augustine, Confessions;611
Notes / Comparative Motifs: Tree as cosmic axis, source of salvation; three trunks: East, West, North; syncretic with Gnostic and Buddhist motifs.

Core Narrative & Theme: Scythian art depicts sacred trees, often flanked by animals or spirits, symbolizing life, kingship, and the world axis. These trees are associated with gold, fertility, and shamanic rites. The motif continues in Central Asian and Iranian traditions2.
Primary Source(s) & Citations: Archaeological finds (Altai, Pazyryk kurgans); Iranian parallels in Avesta2
Notes / Comparative Motifs: Tree flanked by mythical creatures; motif of sacred tree as axis mundi and source of royal/divine power.

Core Narrative & Theme: The Djed pillar, a stylized tree or backbone, symbolizes stability, resurrection, and the cosmic axis. Linked to Osiris and the underworld, sometimes depicted as a literal tree or as a pole with tree-like features9.
Primary Source(s) & Citations: Pyramid Texts; Coffin Texts; temple reliefs9
Notes / Comparative Motifs: Djed as tree/pillar of life; merged with other tree of life motifs in Egyptian religion.

Core Narrative & Theme: The Ished tree grows in Heliopolis; gods inscribe royal names on its leaves. The sycamore and persea are sacred as sources of life, food, and rebirth. Goddesses like Hathor and Nut emerge from or dwell in sacred trees, offering sustenance to the dead9.
Primary Source(s) & Citations: Pyramid Texts; Coffin Texts; temple iconography9
Notes / Comparative Motifs: Trees as sources of immortality, nourishment, and divine wisdom; ished tree linked to kingship and fate.

Name of the Tree (Language): Huluppu (Sumerian: huluppu), Cedar (Akkadian: erēnu), Palm (Akkadian: date-palm)
Core Narrative & Theme: The huluppu tree is nurtured by Inanna; sacred trees appear in royal iconography, flanked by genies or animals, symbolizing life, fertility, and cosmic order. The cedar and date palm are especially important, representing sustenance, wisdom, and the axis mundi89.
Primary Source(s) & Citations: “Inanna and the Huluppu Tree”; palace reliefs; Epic of Gilgamesh Tablet 1289
Notes / Comparative Motifs: Tree as axis mundi, source of divine gifts, and symbol of royal authority; cedar and palm merged as tree of life.

Core Narrative & Theme: Siddhartha Gautama attains enlightenment under the Bodhi Tree at Bodh Gaya. The tree becomes the symbol of awakening, spiritual life, and the axis between worlds10.
Notes / Comparative Motifs: Ficus religiosa (sacred fig); site of pilgrimage and meditation; venerated across Buddhist Asia.

Core Narrative & Theme: The Ashvattha (sacred fig) is the cosmic tree with roots above and branches below (Bhagavad Gita 15.1–3). The Kalpavriksha is the wish-fulfilling tree, emerging from the churning of the ocean, granting all desires10.
Notes / Comparative Motifs: Trees as axis mundi, source of immortality, desires, and cosmic order; central in temple and village life.

Core Narrative & Theme: BMAC iconography features stylized sacred trees flanked by animals or deities, symbolizing life, fertility, and the axis between worlds. This motif influenced later Iranian, Indo-Aryan, and Central Asian traditions2.
Notes / Comparative Motifs: Motif of sacred tree as world axis, source of life, and cosmic connection; precursor to later Iranian/Indian motifs.

Era / Date (Approx.): Table: Eurasian Myths of Churning the Ocean and Emergence of Amrita (with Precise Era Stamps)

Core Narrative & Theme: Key Figures / Deities
Notes / Comparative Motifs: Notes / Comparative Motifs

Name of the Tree (Language): Gods (Devas) and demons (Asuras) churn the cosmic Ocean of Milk to obtain amrita, the nectar of immortality. Mount Mandara is the churning rod, Vasuki the rope, Kurma (tortoise) supports the mountain. Poison emerges, which Shiva swallows. Many treasures and finally amrita emerge.
Core Narrative & Theme: Vishnu (Kurma), Shiva, Devas, Asuras, Vasuki, Dhanvantari
Key Figures / Deities: Amrita (nectar of immortality), Kamadhenu (wish-cow), Airavata (elephant), Uchchaihshravas (horse), Kaustubha (jewel), Apsaras, Kalpavriksha (wish tree), Chandra (moon), Varuni, Lakshmi, Halahala (poison), Dhanvantari (physician with amrita pot)
Primary Source(s) & Citations: Mahabharata 1.15–1.19 (c. 400 BCE–c. 400 CE); Vishnu Purana 1.9–1.10 (c. 300–700 CE); Bhagavata Purana 8.5–8.12 (c. 800–1000 CE)
Notes / Comparative Motifs: Most detailed and influential version; depicted at Angkor Wat. Symbolizes cosmic struggle, cooperation, and the quest for immortality.

Era / Date (Approx.): c. 800 CE – c. 1000 CE (text); earlier oral
Name of the Tree (Language): In the Bundahishn, the primordial sea Vourukasha is churned by the wind to produce the white haoma (sacred drink, source of immortality and health).
Core Narrative & Theme: Ahura Mazda, Tishtrya, Haoma
Notes / Comparative Motifs: Haoma closely parallels soma/amrita. No direct churning myth, but primordial sea as source of elixir.

Name of the Tree (Language): The gods possess ambrosia and nectar, conferring immortality. No direct churning myth, but some scholars compare the motif of divine food/drink emerging from cosmic or primordial sources.
Core Narrative & Theme: Zeus, Hera, other Olympians
Notes / Comparative Motifs: Ambrosia/nectar as divine elixir; motif compared to amrita by Dumézil and others.

Name of the Tree (Language): The divine mead, source of wisdom and poetic inspiration, is created from the blood of Kvasir, mixed with honey, and guarded by giants. Odin steals it for the gods and worthy mortals.
Core Narrative & Theme: Odin, Kvasir, Suttung, Gunnlöð
Notes / Comparative Motifs: Mead as elixir of wisdom and immortality; motif of cosmic quest for a precious substance.

Era / Date (Approx.): c. 4th century BCE – c. 2nd century BCE (earliest ref.); Journey to the West c. 1592 CE
Name of the Tree (Language): Immortality-granting peaches grow in the garden of the Queen Mother of the West (Xi Wangmu). Gods gather for the Peach Banquet every 3,000 years to eat the fruit and gain immortality.
Core Narrative & Theme: Xi Wangmu, Jade Emperor, Eight Immortals
Primary Source(s) & Citations: Shan Hai Jing (Classic of Mountains and Seas, c. 4th–2nd century BCE); Journey to the West ch. 5 (c. 1592 CE)
Notes / Comparative Motifs: No churning, but primordial fruit of immortality from a cosmic garden/oceanic mountain.

Name of the Tree (Language): Inanna finds the huluppu tree in the primordial waters, which yields treasures and is inhabited by divine beings.
Core Narrative & Theme: Inanna, Anzu-bird, Lilith
Primary Source(s) & Citations: Inanna and the Huluppu Tree (c. 2000–1700 BCE); Epic of Gilgamesh Tablet 12 (c. 1200 BCE)
Notes / Comparative Motifs: Primordial waters as source of divine gifts; not a direct churning, but similar emergence motif.

Name of the Tree (Language): From the primeval waters (Nun), a lotus or mound emerges, giving birth to the sun god and life.
Core Narrative & Theme: Atum, Nun, Ra
Primary Source(s) & Citations: Pyramid Texts, Utterance 600 (c. 2300–2100 BCE); Coffin Texts, Spell 76 (c. 2100–1600 BCE)
Notes / Comparative Motifs: No churning, but emergence of life and treasures from primordial ocean.

Core Narrative & Theme: Core Narrative & Theme
Notes / Comparative Motifs: Notes / Comparative Motifs

Core Narrative & Theme: Tree as axis mundi, symbol of fertility and divine connection; earliest symbolic depictions at Domuztepe
Notes / Comparative Motifs: Among the oldest tree of life motifs; tree as link between earth, sky, underworld

Core Narrative & Theme: Acacia and sycamore as trees of rebirth and passage to the afterlife; Osiris and Nut associated with these trees
Notes / Comparative Motifs: Acacia: rebirth, Sycamore: nourishment/protection in afterlife

Core Narrative & Theme: Huluppu tree grows by the Euphrates; Inanna nurtures it, beings inhabit it, Gilgamesh later uses its wood
Primary Source(s) & Citations: “Inanna and the Huluppu Tree”; Epic of Gilgamesh Tablet 12
Notes / Comparative Motifs: Tree as source of life, cosmic order, and divine gifts

Core Narrative & Theme: Sacred tree/pole as symbol of fertility and goddess worship; menorah as stylized almond tree in Temple
Notes / Comparative Motifs: Tree flanked by ibexes; menorah as tree of life in later Jewish tradition

Core Narrative & Theme: Stylized tree attended by genies/kings; axis mundi and symbol of divine blessing, fertility, and kingship
Notes / Comparative Motifs: Iconic, geometric tree-no direct name in texts, but central in royal ideology

Name of the Tree (Language): Tree of the Hesperides (Greek: δένδρον των Εσπερίδων)
Core Narrative & Theme: Golden apple tree in Hera’s garden, guarded by Ladon; apples grant immortality, sought by gods and heroes
Notes / Comparative Motifs: Garden of the Hesperides likened to Eden; apples as symbols of immortality

Core Narrative & Theme: Sacred tree at center of settlement; axis mundi, source of wisdom, gateway to otherworld, symbol of harmony
Notes / Comparative Motifs: Oak, ash, or birch commonly venerated; cutting enemy’s tree seen as spiritual attack

Name of the Tree (Language): Tree of Life (Greek: ξύλον της ζωής)
Core Narrative & Theme: Tree of Life as source of gnosis and salvation; helps Adam attain knowledge, identified with Jesus
Notes / Comparative Motifs: Tree as cosmic axis and source of spiritual enlightenment

Core Narrative & Theme: World ash tree connecting nine worlds; source of fate, wisdom, and cosmic order; gods assemble at its roots
Notes / Comparative Motifs: Ash tree; roots in underworld, branches in heavens; apples of Iðunn grant immortality

Era / Date (Approx.): c. 4th century BCE – c. 2nd century BCE; Journey to the West c. 1592 CE
Name of the Tree (Language): Fusang (扶桑), Peach Tree (桃树 Táoshù)
Core Narrative & Theme: Fusang: mythical mulberry tree in the east; Peach Tree of Immortality in Xi Wangmu’s garden, fruit grants immortality
Key Figures / Deities: Xi Wangmu, Jade Emperor, Eight Immortals
Primary Source(s) & Citations: Shan Hai Jing; Journey to the West57
Notes / Comparative Motifs: Peaches ripen every 3,000 years; tree often guarded by spirits or animals

Era / Date (Approx.): c. 563 BCE – c. 483 BCE (lifetime of Buddha)
Core Narrative & Theme: Buddha attains enlightenment under the Bodhi Tree at Bodh Gaya; tree becomes symbol of awakening and spiritual life
Notes / Comparative Motifs: Ficus religiosa (sacred fig); site of pilgrimage and meditation

Core Narrative & Theme: World tree connects earth, sky, and underworld; source of life, fertility, and divine favor
Notes / Comparative Motifs: Central in Turkic, Siberian, and Finno-Ugric mythologies; often depicted with birds or animals at branches/roots

Name of the Tree (Language): The Great Tree (Oneida: unknown, Ojibwe: Nookomis Giizhig, Lakota: Wakȟáŋ Čháŋ)
Core Narrative & Theme: Tree in Sky World, source of light and fruit; fall of Sky Woman leads to creation of earth on turtle’s back
Primary Source(s) & Citations: Iroquois oral tradition; Black Elk Speaks; Ojibwe and Lakota stories17
Notes / Comparative Motifs: Tree of life as axis mundi and source of creation; central to cosmology and ritual
